Q2 2026 · HOME IMPROVEMENT EDITION
Answer Share:
Home Improvement
AI Has Already Answered. Does Your Brand Show Up?
Powered by BrandRank.AI℠ · Q2 2026
Start with Full Index to explore all 40 questions, or go to Executive Summary for key findings.
See The Answer Divide for brand content analysis and Prompt Considerations to plan your next benchmark.
Executive Summary
Home Depot Dominates — But the Advice Layer Has Defected
This benchmark tested 40 purchase-intent and advice questions across six major AI engines — ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Grok, Meta/Llama, and Perplexity. The findings reveal a sharp and strategically significant divide: Home Depot dominates the purchase layer with near-unanimous consensus across engines, while YouTube has quietly captured the advice layer entirely — leaving every retailer, including Home Depot, absent from the moment consumers decide whether to act. The one exception is Perplexity, which routes advice queries back to Home Depot's own how-to content, offering a proof-of-concept for any brand willing to invest in structured, citable content.
Home Depot's AI Fortress
Home Depot wins ~62% of all purchase-intent answers across all six engines — unanimous on large projects, emergency repairs, lumber, and tools. No competitor has penetrated this AI-encoded dominance, which reflects years of structured content investment and unmatched physical store density.
YouTube Owns the Advice Layer
When consumers ask AI how to do something — fix, build, renovate, learn — YouTube wins 5 of 6 engines per question with the same structural consistency that Home Depot commands in purchasing. No retailer has broken this hold. The brand that bridges YouTube's advice authority with purchase intent will own the full consumer journey.
Challengers Hold Narrow Lanes
Walmart wins basic tools and budget supplies unanimously. Amazon owns fast delivery and small-project convenience. Lowe's surfaces in Gemini and Llama for design-forward renovation. But the moment a question involves scale, urgency, or expertise, all three disappear from AI answers entirely.
Perplexity Is the Proof of Concept
Perplexity is the only engine that routes advice questions to Home Depot's own how-to content library — not YouTube or Reddit. This is proof that structured, citable, expert-attributed content can reclaim the advice layer. The investment required is editorial, not advertising.
45 total questions: 8 Home Projects, 6 Lawn & Garden, 6 Urgency, 6 Small Jobs, 6 Channel Choice, 8 Advice — plus 5 Strategic Depth narratives.
45
Total Questions
ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Grok, Meta/Llama, and Perplexity — each tested with identical prompts in Q2 2026.
6
AI Engines Tested
Home Depot wins ~62% of all Q1–32 purchase-intent questions across all 6 engines. The consensus is near-unanimous for large projects, emergency needs, and lumber.
~62%
Home Depot Win Rate (Q1–32)
In Q33–40, YouTube wins 54% of all 48 advice slots and Reddit 15%. Excluding Perplexity (which routes all 8 answers to HD content), YouTube+Reddit win 83% of remaining slots.
~83%
YouTube/Reddit ex-Perplexity
Home Depot gets ZERO primary advice recommendations from ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Grok, and Llama across all 8 advice questions. Only Perplexity breaks this pattern.
0%
HD in Advice (ex-Perplexity)
Home Depot wins every urgency question unanimously. No competitor has penetrated this AI-encoded fortress — though any inventory failure at scale puts this edge at risk.
HD
Overall Answer Share Leader
Home Depot wins the majority of Q1–32 purchase and urgency questions across all 6 engines with near-unanimous consensus. Every engine agrees on Home Depot for large projects, emergency needs, and lumber — a remarkable alignment signaling strong AI content authority.
This is the most actionable gap in the study. The brand that bridges purchase intent with AI-endorsed advice authority will own the full consumer journey end-to-end.
Split
The Advice Authority Gap
For Q33–40, not a single engine (except Perplexity) recommends Home Depot. YouTube wins 54% of all advice slots — and 65% when Perplexity's HD-routed answers are excluded. Reddit wins ~25%. This is a massive unclaimed territory — and a direct strategic vulnerability for HD.
Retailer Consensus Patterns
Wins all 8 Home Projects, all 6 Urgency questions unanimously. Lumber, tools, one-stop, lawn mowers — near-total consensus.HOME DEPOTDominant for large projects, emergency/urgency, lumber, tools, same-day needs.
Walmart wins Q11, Q21, Q26 unanimously (6/6 engines). Clean lane: basic tools, affordable gardening, inexpensive repair items.WALMARTWins basic/budget tools, small convenience purchases — high consensus in its lane.
Amazon wins fast delivery Q32 (5/6 engines) and small project convenience Q25 (5/6). Strong but bounded by project scale and urgency.AMAZONOwns fast delivery and small project convenience. Loses on scale & urgency.
Lowe's surfaces in Gemini (HP2, HP5, HP8, LG1, LG4, UR6) and Llama (HP2, HP6, HP8, LG2, LG5, UR6) — design-forward and beginner-friendly framing.LOWE'SSurfaces in Gemini & Llama for bathroom, kitchen, lawn — design-skewed positioning.
Advice Channel Ownership
YouTube wins 5/6 engines for Q33, Q35, Q36, Q38. Truly unanimous for visual learning. The "advice layer default" across all AI engines.YOUTUBEUnanimous winner for learning, DIY tutorials, step-by-step guidance.
Reddit wins Q39 (urgent repair) from ChatGPT, Grok, Llama. Also wins Q34 (reliable DIY advice) from Gemini and Llama. Community validation for urgent problems.REDDITWins urgent Q&A and community validation across multiple engines.
Gemini alone surfaces Family Handyman (Q36), Local Extension Office (Q37), Houzz (Q38) — indicating richer alternative-source diversification in Google's model.OTHERSGemini uniquely surfaces Family Handyman, Extension Offices, Houzz.
Perplexity is the ONLY engine routing advice questions back to HD's own how-to library. Proof that structured, citable content can reclaim the advice layer.PERPLEXITYUnique: routes all advice questions to Home Depot's own content library.
Brand Signals
Eight Strategic Insights
BrandRank.AI Signal — The Biggest Vulnerability
Home Depot owns the transaction. It does not own the trust.
AI engines are creating a two-stage consumer journey: the advice stage (YouTube, Reddit) and the purchase stage (Home Depot). If a competitor with strong content authority — or a platform like YouTube itself — can bridge these two stages, Home Depot's transactional dominance becomes vulnerable. The company's complete absence from 5 of 6 engines in Q33–40 advice answers is the single most actionable insight in this study.

Answer Share℠
Who Wins the Prompted Moment of Truth?
Answer Share measures how often each retailer or source is named as the primary answer across all 6 engines for each question. Hover any bar or row for detail.
Engine-by-Engine Answer Share Breakdown
Engine HD# of Q1–40 where Home Depot is the primary answer from this engine. WMT# of questions where Walmart wins from this engine. AMZ# of questions where Amazon wins from this engine. YT# of questions where YouTube wins from this engine. RDT# of questions where Reddit wins from this engine. LOW# of questions where Lowe's wins from this engine. OTHERAll other answers: In-Store, Ace Hardware, Harbor Freight, Local Nursery, Hardware Store, Houzz, etc.
Engine Personas
Six Distinct Lenses on Home Improvement
Each AI engine brings a distinct decision-making lens. Understanding these personas is critical for brands building Answer Economy content strategies. Hover the strength tags for specific question evidence.
ChatGPT
The Pragmatic Operator
Tone: Confident · Project-Focused
Most heavily weighted toward Home Depot. Rarely surfaces niche alternatives. Provides clean, decisive single-answer recommendations. High brand authority for Home Depot and YouTube with little ambiguity. The most "expected" engine in the study.
HD Loyal Recommends HD for all 8 Home Projects, all 6 Urgency questions. Zero exceptions in its core territory. YouTube Routes Q33, Q35, Q36, Q37, Q38, Q40 to YouTube — the full learning stack. Consistent and unanimous. Amazon ~Amazon wins Q22 (convenient repair), Q25 (small project), Q32 (fast delivery) from ChatGPT. Bounded to convenience and speed. No Niche Never recommends Ace Hardware, Harbor Freight, Houzz, Family Handyman, or Local Extension Offices. Zero alternative-source diversity.
Gemini
The Diversified Recommender
Tone: Nuanced · Source-Rich
Most likely to surface non-obvious answers. Lowe's for bathrooms and lawn, Ace Hardware for urgency, Harbor Freight for budget tools, Local Extension Offices for gardening, Houzz for renovation planning. The richest competitive landscape of any engine.
Lowe's Lift Recommends Lowe's in HP2, HP5, HP8, LG1, LG4, UR6 — 6 questions. Consistently frames Lowe's as more design-oriented and beginner-friendly than HD. Niche Sources Family Handyman (Q36), Local Extension Office (Q37), Houzz (Q38), Local Hardware (UR3), Local Nursery (LG6) — unique among all 6 engines. Harbor Freight ~Recommends Harbor Freight for Q28 (best value tools) and Q24 (budget tools). Budget-tier differentiation not seen in any other engine. Reddit Q34Gemini recommends Reddit for Q34 (reliable DIY advice) — peer validation emphasis. One of few non-YouTube advice answers.
Claude
The Balanced Segmenter
Tone: Analytical · Context-Aware
Mirrors ChatGPT for large-project questions but clearly segments by use case. Provides the most nuanced Q41–45 strategic narratives of any engine — explicit about the advice vs. purchase divide and the role of content platforms in the learning layer.
Use-Case Match Clean segmentation: budget/basic→Walmart, speed/online→Amazon, project/scale→HD. The most consistent use-case logic of any engine. Reddit Q40 Claude uniquely recommends Reddit for Q40 (tools/materials advice) — community peer validation over retailer or YouTube. Distinct from all other engines. Lowe's Peer ~Lowe's appears in Claude's Q41–45 narratives as a credible HD peer for design-conscious remodelers. Acknowledged but not primary. Depth Q41–45Claude's Q41–45 responses are the longest and most analytically layered — highest strategic depth score across all engines in the study.
Grok
The HD Maximalist
Tone: Assertive · HD-Dominant
Highest Home Depot win rate of all engines — recommends HD even for pre-renovation advice (Q38), a position no other engine takes. Grok is Home Depot's most powerful AI advocate in the ecosystem. Lowest recommendation diversity.
Max HD Share Grok returns Home Depot as primary answer in the most questions of any engine tested — widest HD coverage across all 6 categories. HD Advice Q38 Grok recommends HD workshops for pre-renovation advice — the ONLY engine to route an advice question to a retailer (other than Perplexity's how-to library). Reddit Urgent ~Grok recommends Reddit for Q39 (urgent repair Q&A) and Q34 (reliable DIY advice) — its only substantive non-HD, non-YouTube answers. No Niche Never recommends Harbor Freight, Ace, Houzz, Extension Offices, or Lowe's. The least diversified engine in the study.
Meta / Llama
The Balanced Generalist
Tone: Even-Handed · Brand-Neutral
Most balanced engine — the only one giving Lowe's and HD roughly equal weight. Amazon wins more urgency questions here than in other engines. Reliable but lowest-drama, least distinctive recommendations in the study.
Lowe's Parity Recommends Lowe's in HP2, HP6, HP8, LG2, LG5, UR3, UR6 — the most Lowe's wins of any engine (7 questions). Near parity with HD in several categories. Amazon Urgency ~Llama routes Q16 (fastest tools today) to Amazon — unique; all other engines default to HD or Ace. A subtle but notable urgency divergence. Balanced SplitNo strong directional lean — the most even HD/Lowe's distribution of any engine, making it the most "neutral" in the study. Low DepthLlama's Q41–45 responses are the least detailed — confirms narrative rather than interrogates it. Lowest strategic depth score.
Perplexity
The Citation-Backed Conformist
Tone: Source-Heavy · HD-Aligned
Very high HD win rate — but uniquely routes ALL advice questions to Home Depot's own how-to content. This is the most strategically significant finding: Perplexity is the only engine where owned content reclaims the advice layer entirely.
HD How-To Routes all 8 advice questions (Q33–Q40) to HD's project library and how-to guides — the only engine to do so. HD's 12 total advice wins in this study come entirely from Perplexity. Citation-Driven Citation architecture means any structured, citable, expert-attributed content gets surfaced. The most content-strategy-responsive engine in the study. Advice Breach Proof-of-concept that a retailer CAN win the advice layer — if it publishes structured how-to content. A template any brand can replicate. Weak NuanceStrong on what (citations) but weak on why — Q41–45 qualitative responses are the least analytically rich despite citation depth.
Strategic Depth · Q41–45
Where AI Engines Build the Purchase Framework
Questions 41–45 required narrative responses — revealing how AI engines frame the shopping journey and which brands earn lasting narrative authority in the answer layer.
Q41 — Key Factors When Deciding Where to Buy
ChatGPT
Project scale, urgency, expertise, and budget are key. HD and Lowe's lead for large projects; Walmart wins everyday convenience; Amazon excels in delivery and niche items.
Gemini
Proximity, inventory depth, and expertise matter most. HD/Lowe's lead project-scale selection; Ace wins on service; Walmart and Amazon dominate price for low-complexity items.
Perplexity
People weigh selection, price, speed, and advice. Home Depot leads for project depth; Lowe's close; Walmart for basics; Amazon for fast delivery and easy ordering.
Q42 — What Makes an Advice Source Trustworthy?
ChatGPT
Trust builds through demonstrated expertise, visual proof, and community validation. YouTube channels earn credibility through transparency. Reddit offers peer-tested unbiased recommendations.
Gemini
Trust stems from expert credentials, visual proof, and peer review. YouTube for visual learners; Reddit for troubleshooting; Family Handyman for professional standards.
Grok
Trustworthy advice is accurate, specific, and demonstrated. YouTube pros and manufacturer sites are strongest; Reddit with photos adds credibility. Avoid unverified TikTok trends.
Q43 — Online vs. In-Store: The Decision Logic
Claude
In-store for urgent needs, large items, and expert guidance. Online for convenience, price comparison, niche products. Amazon dominates planned small-item online orders.
Gemini
Online wins for research and small non-urgent items. In-store wins for hands-on items like lumber or paint and urgent needs requiring immediate expert consultation.
Perplexity
Online for speed, comparison, small items. In-store for bulky materials, immediate needs, hands-on inspection. Amazon/Walmart for basics; HD/Lowe's for specialty inventory.
Q44 — DIY vs. Hire a Professional
ChatGPT
DIY wins for visual, low-risk tasks. Professionals needed for electrical, structural, plumbing. YouTube and Reddit increasingly empower borderline DIYers to attempt more.
Grok
DIY if simple, skilled, or budget-tight — tutorials help. Hire pros for complex electrical/plumbing. Start small, scale up confidence and tool ownership progressively.
Llama
Decision depends on complexity, skill level, and time. People seek YouTube and Reddit advice to assess their capability before committing to DIY.
Q45 — How Do the Major Retailers Compare? (Cross-Engine Synthesis)
ChatGPT
HD targets serious DIYers and pros with deep inventory, tool rentals, project services. Lowe's skews toward homeowners. Walmart captures convenience/budget. Amazon dominates online replenishment but lacks in-person expertise.
Gemini
HD targets pros and heavy DIYers. Lowe's focuses on home aesthetics. Walmart serves low-cost casual needs. Amazon provides unmatched convenience and broad selection but lacks immediate-need fulfillment.
Claude
HD leads for large complex projects requiring variety and guidance. Walmart wins everyday price. Amazon excels in niche items and delivery speed. Lowe's competes closely, appealing to design-conscious remodelers.
Grok
HD excels with pro/DIY bulk, workshops, and rentals. Lowe's similar with upscale appliances. Walmart for cheap basics. Amazon for vast selection and fast shipping but less expertise.
Llama
HD and Lowe's specialize in home improvement with wide selections and expert advice. Walmart offers affordable basics. Amazon provides fast shipping with wide but varied selection and no in-person expertise.
Perplexity
HD and Lowe's focus on specialized home improvement breadth and project services. Walmart is strongest for low-cost basics. Amazon is strongest for speed, breadth of listings, and easy online ordering.
The Answer Divide℠
Curiosity-Led vs. Conversion-Led
We typed "how to build a patio" into every major platform in this study. What came back reveals everything about how each brand has — or hasn't — invested in the advice layer. The brands AI recommends most are the ones that answer first and sell second. The ones that lead with product catalogs are invisible to AI when a consumer is still in learning mode.
The Curiosity-to-Conversion Spectrum
Curiosity-Led
Hybrid
Conversion-Led
YouTube
Pure Advice
Reddit
Peer Trust
Amazon / Rufus
Diagnostic AI
Home Depot
Answer + Cart
Lowe's
Guide + Buy
Ace Hardware
Product Only
Harbor Freight
No Intent Match
Walmart
Catalog Only
Curiosity-Led
Home Depot
Answer Platform
Home Depot leads with a full how-to video library organized by project category — "How to Build a Paver Patio," Outdoor Living guides, 78 tutorials just in that section. A consumer asking "how to build a patio" lands immediately in step-by-step instructional content before a single product is pushed. The DIY nav item in the main header signals a brand that has structurally committed to the advice layer. This is precisely why Perplexity routes advice questions back to HD content — it is citable, structured, and genuinely educational.
AI VERDICT: Earns the purchase by answering the question first
Curiosity-Led
Lowe's
Guide + Commerce
Lowe's delivers a well-structured buying guide — 'Types of Pavers: A Patio Block and Yard Pavers Buying Guide' — complete with author attribution, a table of contents, and a proper article format. This is stronger than most retailers. But the framing is 'buying guide' rather than 'how-to guide' — the conversion intent is embedded in the premise. The consumer who wants to understand before they buy is partially served; the one who wants to learn the skill first is redirected toward purchase.
AI VERDICT: Informs the purchase — but frames it as a purchase from the start
Conversion-Led
Walmart
Commerce Engine
"How to build a patio" returns 237 product results for patio furniture sets, fire pit tables, and outdoor chairs — starting at $66. There is no how-to content, no guide, no educational layer. Walmart has interpreted a project-intent query as a product-purchase query and served its catalog. For a consumer genuinely trying to learn, this is a dead end. This behavior is consistent with why AI engines never route advice questions to Walmart — there is nothing to cite.
AI VERDICT: Misses the intent entirely — catalog over curiosity
Hybrid
Amazon / Rufus
Diagnostic AI Commerce
Amazon's Rufus AI assistant is the most interesting case in this study. When asked 'how to build a patio,' Rufus leads with planning guidance — 'here are essential materials and tools' — before organizing results into Planning & Construction Guide books and Patio Foundation Materials. It is using AI to bridge curiosity and conversion in real time, recommending a Black & Decker guide book alongside paver molds. Rufus is structurally closer to curiosity-led than the Amazon catalog alone — but the endpoint is always a product add-to-cart.
AI VERDICT: Diagnostic AI — qualifies intent, builds understanding, earns the purchase
Conversion-Led
Ace Hardware
Product Catalog
"How to build a patio" returns 2 results — both Halo LED outdoor light fixtures. Ace has indexed the query against product inventory and returned an almost comically irrelevant result. This is not a content strategy failure; it is the absence of one. Ace's AI visibility in our study (Gemini's urgency and convenience recommendations) is earned through store proximity, not content authority. In the advice layer, Ace is invisible — and this screenshot shows why.
AI VERDICT: No content strategy = no advice layer presence
Conversion-Led
Harbor Freight
Catalog Without Context
"How to build a patio" returns 200 items — Earth Augers, Portable Canopies, Light-Duty Tarps, Garden Hoses, Wagons, Solar panels, and Welding Tables. The category suggestions bear almost no relationship to the intent of the query. Harbor Freight has indexed the words but completely missed the meaning. There is no how-to content, no project guide, no planning layer — just a product dump that treats a learning intent as a shopping trigger. This is precisely why Harbor Freight only surfaces in Gemini for budget-tool questions, where its positioning is price-specific and narrow. Any content investment would be a direct AI visibility unlock.
AI VERDICT: Keyword match with zero intent match — invisible in the advice layer
Curiosity-Led
YouTube
Pure Advice Authority
YouTube returns exactly what every AI engine knows: the top result is a 22:58 video from Handyman Startup — "How to build your dream paver patio (and save $10K+)" with 334K views — followed by Shorts from creators with 2.4M, 188K, and 1.1M views. There is no commerce layer, no add-to-cart, no conversion pressure. The only goal is to teach. This is why YouTube wins 5 of 6 engines per advice question. It is the purest expression of curiosity-led content in the home improvement category — and it is not a retailer.
AI VERDICT: The gold standard — pure teaching with zero conversion pressure
Curiosity-Led
Reddit
Peer Validation Layer
"How to build a patio" surfaces r/landscaping posts from real homeowners: "I Built a Patio Last Year Without any Experience" (2K votes), "New patio build in backyard DIY" (82 votes), "I build a patio in my backyard, saved $10,000 by doing it myself" (6.6K votes). This is unfiltered peer testimony — the ultimate trust signal for a hesitant DIYer. Reddit does not sell anything. It validates the decision to act. This is why AI routes urgent and peer-validation advice questions here, and why Reddit's advice authority is structurally immune to retailer competition.
AI VERDICT: Community trust — impossible for any retailer to replicate
The Answer Divide℠ — What This Means
The brands AI trusts most are the ones that answer before they sell.
Every brand in this study typed the same query — "how to build a patio" — into their own search bar. The results tell the whole story. YouTube taught. Reddit validated. Home Depot showed you how, then offered the supplies. Lowe's offered a buying guide. Amazon's Rufus offered a book and some materials. Ace and Walmart showed you products that had nothing to do with the question. The AI engines in our benchmark did not choose these brands randomly. They chose the brands whose content most reliably answers the consumer's actual question. The Answer Divide℠ is not a content philosophy debate. It is a measurable, rankable difference in AI visibility — and the gap between curiosity-led and conversion-led brands will only widen as AI answer engines become the dominant interface for consumer discovery.
Data is the property of BrandRank.AI℠. If used for commercial purposes and you are not a current client, please request permission before use.
Prompt Considerations
100 Additional Prompts to Deepen the Analysis
The 40 questions in this benchmark establish a strong baseline — but the most strategically valuable insights often emerge from the next layer of prompting. Each category below includes the learning opportunity so brands understand where the analysis could take them before committing to a full run.
Category A · Large Projects & Materials
Learning Opportunity
Home Depot's dominance in baseline project questions is well established. The next layer reveals whether that dominance holds for specialized materials — custom millwork, roofing, flooring, windows — or whether specialty retailers and manufacturer direct channels begin to surface. Brands supplying these categories (Trex, Andersen, Armstrong) should watch carefully for any engine that breaks from the HD default.
1. Where should I buy materials for a basement finishing project?
2. What's the best place to get custom millwork and trim?
3. Where should I buy flooring for a whole-house renovation?
4. What's the best place to source tile for a large bathroom project?
5. Where should I buy roofing materials for a DIY repair?
6. Where can I find the best selection of kitchen cabinets?
7. Where do I buy energy-efficient windows and exterior doors?
8. What's the best retailer for insulation and weatherproofing supplies?
Category B · Brand & Product Specific
Learning Opportunity
Generic category questions favor the dominant retailer. Brand-specific questions reveal something far more interesting: whether AI engines route consumers to the manufacturer directly, to a preferred retail channel, or to a price-comparison destination. This is critical intelligence for brands like DeWalt, Moen, Kohler, and Sherwin-Williams that sell through multiple channels and need to understand which channel AI endorses — and why.
9. Where is the best place to buy DeWalt power tools?
10. What retailer has the best selection of Kohler fixtures?
11. Where should I buy Benjamin Moore paint?
12. Where can I find the best deals on Milwaukee Tool products?
13. Which retailer carries the most Trex decking products?
14. What's the best place to buy Moen faucets and fixtures?
15. Where can I compare Behr vs Sherwin-Williams paint side by side?
16. Where do I buy a Nest or Ecobee smart thermostat at the best price?
Category C · Seasonal & Outdoor Living
Learning Opportunity
Seasonal questions introduce timing and urgency signals that can shift AI routing dramatically. When a consumer asks "where do I buy a snowblower before winter" or "where can I get a gas grill for summer," does AI default to physical store availability or pivot to Amazon for pre-season ordering? This category can expose whether AI engines are seasonally aware — and whether outdoor living brands like Weber, Husqvarna, or Suncast have any AI visibility of their own.
17. Where should I buy a snowblower before winter hits?
18. What's the best place to buy a gas grill for summer?
19. Where can I find outdoor string lighting for a patio renovation?
20. What's the best retailer for a backyard storage shed?
21. Where should I buy irrigation supplies for a sprinkler system?
22. What's the best place to buy pressure washing equipment?
23. Where can I get supplies for building a raised garden bed?
24. Where should I buy pool supplies and maintenance chemicals?
Category D · Smart Home & Technology
Learning Opportunity
Smart home questions sit at the intersection of home improvement and consumer electronics — a channel battleground where Best Buy, Amazon, and Apple compete directly with Home Depot and Lowe's. AI routing in this category is likely to be far less settled than in core home improvement, potentially surfacing manufacturer websites, Amazon, or tech retailers as primary answers. For brands like Ring, Google Nest, or EV charger makers, this is a critical visibility test.
25. What's the best place to buy smart home devices?
26. Where should I buy a video doorbell like Ring or Nest?
27. What retailer has the best selection of smart lighting?
28. Where can I buy a Level 2 EV charger for my home garage?
29. What's the best place to buy a whole-home standby generator?
30. Where should I go for home security cameras and systems?
31. Where can I buy a smart irrigation controller for my yard?
32. What's the best retailer for home automation hubs and ecosystems?
Category E · Pro & Contractor Behavior
Learning Opportunity
Professional and contractor-intent questions are among the highest-value queries in home improvement — and the AI routing may diverge significantly from consumer answers. Does AI route contractors to HD Pro Desk, Fastenal, Ferguson, or Grainger? Do trade-specific questions (electricians, plumbers, painters) surface trade supply houses over big-box retail? This category directly maps to B2B revenue opportunity and could reveal whether Home Depot's pro business has the same AI authority as its consumer business.
33. Where do professional contractors buy supplies?
34. What's the best place to open a contractor account for trade discounts?
35. Where should a general contractor buy lumber in volume?
36. What's the best retailer for commercial-grade power tools?
37. Where can a plumber source fixtures and pipes at trade pricing?
38. What's the best place for electricians to buy wire, panels, and breakers?
39. Where do painters buy supplies and coatings in bulk?
40. What's the best retailer for job site safety equipment and PPE?
Category F · Trust, Advice & Learning
Learning Opportunity
Our baseline study confirmed YouTube's stranglehold on the advice layer. But the next layer of advice questions can reveal whether that hold is universal or varies by trade type, urgency, skill level, and medium. Which YouTube channels get specifically named? Does TikTok surface as an alternative? Does AI ever recommend calling a professional over watching a video? These questions map the full topology of the advice layer and reveal which content investments are most likely to generate AI visibility for brands willing to compete in the trust economy.
41. Which YouTube channel is best for DIY home repair beginners?
42. What subreddit is most helpful for home improvement questions?
43. Where should I go to learn how to tile a bathroom floor?
44. What's the best app for planning a home renovation?
45. Where can I find unbiased reviews of home improvement products?
46. Who gives better advice — a store associate or YouTube?
47. What's the best podcast for home improvement and renovation tips?
48. Where can I find AI-powered guidance for home repair decisions?
Category G · Price, Value & Comparison
Learning Opportunity
Price and value questions are the most likely to surface Amazon as a primary recommendation — and the most likely to reveal which engines are price-sensitive vs. authority-sensitive in their routing logic. They also expose whether AI treats price comparison as a destination (Google Shopping, Wirecutter) or defaults to a trusted retailer. For brands whose margins depend on avoiding a race to the bottom, understanding how AI handles value-framing is a critical vulnerability assessment.
49. Where can I get the best price on power tools right now?
50. Is it cheaper to buy home improvement supplies online or in-store?
51. What's the best way to save money on a bathroom remodel?
52. Where can I find coupons or discounts for home improvement stores?
53. Is Home Depot or Lowe's cheaper for lumber?
54. Where is the best place to buy refurbished or used power tools?
55. What's the best time of year to buy appliances at the lowest price?
56. Where do I find the best deals on end-of-season outdoor furniture?
Category H · Sustainability & Energy Efficiency
Learning Opportunity
Sustainability and green home improvement is one of the fastest-growing consumer intent categories — and likely one of the least settled in AI routing. Questions about solar, heat pumps, green insulation, and energy-efficient appliances may route to specialty installers, utility rebate programs, government sites, or Consumer Reports rather than any retailer. Brands with strong sustainability positioning (Rheem, Generac, SunPower) should audit this layer urgently. The AI answer in this category is being written now — and the brands who invest in citable content first will own it.
57. Where should I buy solar panels for my home?
58. What's the best place to buy a heat pump water heater?
59. Where can I find Energy Star certified appliances?
60. What's the best retailer for eco-friendly building materials?
61. Where do I buy green insulation for an attic retrofit?
62. What's the best way to find government rebates for home energy upgrades?
63. Where can I buy a tankless water heater for maximum efficiency?
64. What's the best place to buy recycled or reclaimed lumber?
Category I · Rental, Services & Installation
Learning Opportunity
This category tests the frontier between product and service — and is likely to produce the most fragmented AI routing of any category in the study. When a consumer asks "where can I rent a tile saw" or "who should I hire to install my flooring," the answer could go to Home Depot's rental center, Sunbelt Rentals, Angi, TaskRabbit, or local contractor directories. For service marketplace brands and tool rental companies, this is entirely uncharted AI territory — and among the highest conversion-value prompts in home improvement.
65. Where can I rent a tile saw for a weekend project?
66. Who should I hire to install my new flooring?
67. What's the best way to find a reliable local plumber?
68. Where can I find a vetted contractor for a kitchen remodel?
69. What's the best app for booking a handyman quickly?
70. Where can I rent heavy equipment for a landscaping project?
71. What's the best place to buy an extended warranty for appliances?
72. Where can I get a free estimate for a home renovation project?
Category J · Safety, Code & Compliance
Learning Opportunity
Safety and code compliance questions represent the highest-stakes advice layer in home improvement — and the most likely to route away from commercial sources entirely. When a consumer asks "do I need a permit to build a deck" or "what's the electrical code for a bathroom," AI may route to government sites, NFPA, local building departments, or legal/professional services rather than any retailer. For brands with strong safety or code compliance content — or brands whose products require professional installation — this category reveals a trust gap that can only be closed with authoritative, citable content that AI considers credible.
73. Do I need a permit to build a deck in my backyard?
74. What's the electrical code for adding an outlet in a bathroom?
75. How do I know if I need a licensed contractor for a home project?
76. What safety precautions should I take when working with electrical wiring?
77. Where can I find local building codes for a home addition?
78. Is it safe to DIY my own plumbing repairs or should I hire a pro?
79. What are the load-bearing wall rules I need to know before renovating?
80. Where can I learn about asbestos testing before a home renovation?
Category K · Design, Inspiration & Planning
Learning Opportunity
Design and inspiration questions are where Houzz, Pinterest, Architectural Digest, and HGTV compete with Home Depot and Lowe's for AI real estate — and where neither retailer has strong structural authority. This category tests whether AI routes design-intent questions to media brands, social platforms, or retailers, and specifically whether Lowe's design-forward positioning (observed in our baseline study) extends into the inspiration layer. For interior design brands, paint companies, and fixture manufacturers, understanding AI's design authority routing is a high-priority content strategy input.
81. Where can I find inspiration for a bathroom remodel?
82. What's the best app for visualizing paint colors in my home?
83. Where should I go for kitchen design ideas before a renovation?
84. What's the best website for home design inspiration?
85. Where can I find a free room planner tool online?
86. Who has better home design tools — Home Depot or Lowe's?
87. Where can I find trending exterior paint color ideas?
88. What's the best platform for finding a local interior designer?
Category L · Head-to-Head Brand Battles
Learning Opportunity
Direct comparison prompts are among the most commercially valuable questions a brand can test — and the most revealing of AI bias or authority signals. When a consumer asks "Home Depot vs Lowe's" or "DeWalt vs Milwaukee," AI engines must take a position, either explicitly or through their routing choice. These prompts reveal how AI frames competitive differentiation, whether it has a preferred brand narrative, and whether that narrative aligns with or undermines your brand's positioning. For any brand in a head-to-head competitive market, these twelve questions are mandatory in any AI visibility audit.
89. Home Depot vs Lowe's — which is better for a kitchen renovation?
90. DeWalt vs Milwaukee — which power tools are better for DIY?
91. Amazon vs Home Depot — where should I buy home improvement supplies?
92. Behr vs Sherwin-Williams — which paint brand does AI recommend?
93. Moen vs Delta — which faucet brand is recommended most by AI?
94. Trex vs Fiberon — which composite decking does AI prefer?
95. Home Depot vs Ace Hardware — which is better for emergency repairs?
96. YouTube vs TikTok — where does AI send people for DIY tutorials?
97. Lowe's vs Menards — which retailer does AI recommend in the Midwest?
98. Home Depot vs Fastenal — where do AI engines send professional contractors?
99. Houzz vs Pinterest — which platform does AI recommend for design inspiration?
100. Reddit vs Family Handyman — which advice source does AI trust more for repairs?
